# 1. Introduction

Climate change is reshaping the conditions under which the world's food is produced. The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change has established that the accumulation of greenhouse gases is driving significant changes in Earth's climate [@ipcc2007; @ipcc2013], and these changes threaten agriculture through rising temperatures [@lobell2011], shifting precipitation patterns [@yuan2024], more frequent extreme weather [@lesk2016], and dwindling water supplies [@liu2022]. For the major feed grains, empirical work has consistently linked these shifts to marked yield declines [@schlenker2009; @tack2015; @zhang2017; @shew2020; @malikov2020]. Far less is known about how the same forces affect pasture and forage, the feed base on which grazing livestock depend, although the limited evidence available points to adverse effects [@gauly2013].

This gap matters because the United States livestock industry, among the largest in the world, depends heavily on a stable supply of feed grains and forage crops [@simmons1966; @marsh2007], making domestic forage a matter of national rather than merely regional consequence. Warming presses on this system from several directions at once. Longer and more severe droughts can force ranchers toward alternative feed sources [@kemper2013], a pressure compounded by competition between livestock and humans for major grain crops [@mottet2017], with implications for domestic food availability and exports through both direct grain consumption and its indirect consumption as livestock feed. Warming may also redraw the geography of forage production, raising the cost of sourcing and transporting feed for producers who use it to supplement or replace grazing [@kemper2013]. These risks are not hypothetical: the 2012 nationwide drought degraded more than 70% of U.S. pasturelands and cut cow-calf returns by more than \$100 per cow [@kemper2013]. Warming also acts on the animals themselves, changing water use [@ahlberg2018], production [@chen2024; @summer2019], reproduction [@wolfenson2019], and the mix of available forage [@xie2022]: daily water consumption in beef cattle rises by about 0.65 kg for each degree Celsius above roughly 25°C, heat stress lowers feed intake and with it milk and beef output, and dairy conception rates fall by an additional 5% when summer temperatures run 1.5°C above average [@wolfenson2019]. As conditions warm, the forage base itself tends to shift from cool-season (C3) toward warm-season (C4) species better suited to hotter, drier conditions [@xie2022].

Alfalfa is the natural focus for this problem. It ranks as the fourth-largest U.S. crop by acreage [@usda_quickstats]. It is prized for its high protein content and nitrogen-fixing ability, which reduces reliance on synthetic fertilizers.^[An acre of alfalfa, on average, will fix about 200 kg of nitrogen per year.] In 2019 the United States harvested more than 54 million tons of alfalfa worth roughly \$8.4 billion, part of a hay sector spanning about 128 million acres and valued near \$17 billion [@usda_quickstats]. Production is concentrated in the northwestern United States, especially California, Idaho, Nevada, Oregon, Wyoming, and Washington (Figure 1), a geographic concentration that itself signals exposure to climatic change. Yet despite alfalfa's standing as a primary protein source for the nation's cattle, the tools used to quantify climate impacts on field crops have not been brought to bear on it. The endogenous piecewise degree-day framework that underpins much of the grain-yield literature was developed for and applied to grain crops [@tack2015; @schlenker2009], and climate-focused yield analysis for forage remains sparse relative to field crops [@zhang2017; @shew2020; @malikov2020]. County-level estimates of the heat thresholds and yield penalties specific to U.S. alfalfa are absent from the published literature, and while related work documents climate risks to dairy systems, it is set in Central Europe rather than the United States [@gauly2013]. As a result, the forage and livestock-feed supply chain, and how warming might disrupt it, has been left largely unexamined.

This study addresses this gap by examining two related questions: whether warming has a measurable effect on alfalfa hay yields, and whether projected warming will alter the crop's spatial distribution. To investigate these questions, a county-level panel spanning 1951 to 2022 is assembled, and established models of the temperature-yield relationship [@schlenker2009; @tack2015; @shew2020] are applied to estimate the temperature thresholds above which alfalfa yields begin to decline and the rate at which yields decrease as exposure to harmful heat increases. These estimates are then used to simulate how uniform warming scenarios may redistribute alfalfa production across the United States.

Three principal findings emerge. First, the temperature-yield relationship is strongly nonlinear: exposure to beneficial heat between `r fmt_degC(objs$knots$lower)` and `r fmt_degC(objs$knots$upper)` is positively associated with alfalfa yields, while exposure above `r fmt_degC(objs$knots$upper)` is detrimental. Second, uniform warming results in progressively greater yield losses, ranging from mild to severe as warming increases from 0.5°C to 3°C. Third, the impacts are geographically heterogeneous and correspond to agro-climatic zones. Cooler counties in the Northeast, such as Connecticut, Rhode Island, and Massachusetts, tend to benefit as warming reduces heat stress, whereas the South and parts of the Midwest and West experience declines, with the most pronounced losses in Texas, Louisiana, Oklahoma, Kansas, and Arizona. Even a 0.5°C increase raises yields in some northern states but lowers them in the South, where Florida experiences a `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact_state$FL$s05, 2)` decline; under a 3°C warming scenario, losses intensify to `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact_state$FL$s30, 2)` in Florida and `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact_state$TX$s30, 2)` in Texas, and most northern gains diminish or reverse beyond approximately 2°C. Sensitivity to harmful heat is greatest in the hot, humid South and nearly absent in the heavily irrigated Southwest, although this apparent resilience is conditional on continued irrigation.

The consequences extend beyond the field. Because county cattle inventory is dependent on local forage, a 1°C warming reduces alfalfa availability by `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact_avail$s10)` and cattle inventory by `r fmt_abs_pct(objs$impact_cattle$s10)` on average. This effect is most pronounced in the livestock-dense South and Plains, further widening the gap between regions where forage can be produced and where feed is required.

These patterns indicate that warming will not only reduce alfalfa yields but also shift production away from regions most reliant on domestic forage. The steepest declines align with the most livestock-dense areas, which already subject animals to direct heat stress, suggesting that pressures on feed supply and livestock herds are likely to intensify concurrently. Producers experiencing local forage deficits may incur higher costs to import feed or may need to transition to warm-season forages more suited to elevated temperatures. Such adjustments involve agronomic and nutritional trade-offs. These findings underscore a growing mismatch between forage supply and feed demand, highlighting the need for targeted regional adaptation strategies, as discussed further in the subsequent sections.

This study makes three primary contributions. Methodologically, it is the first to apply the endogenous piecewise degree-day framework, previously used for grain crops [@tack2015; @schlenker2009], to U.S. alfalfa hay, utilizing the most comprehensive dataset assembled for the crop, encompassing over 70 years of county-level data from `r fmt_num(objs$summary$n_counties, 0)` counties. Empirically, the study provides county-level yield-shock simulations across six warming scenarios, offering the regional and state-level detail necessary for local supply-chain planning, and directly links forage outcomes to the livestock sector, where county cattle inventory and alfalfa production are correlated at `r fmt_num(objs$assoc$cor_production, 2)`. Additionally, by comparing alfalfa with non-alfalfa hay, the study provides empirical evidence of differential climate sensitivity across forage types, a distinction not previously addressed in the literature. In doing so, the study fills the county-level gap in heat-threshold and yield-penalty estimates for U.S. alfalfa that prior research, focused on grains or other regions [@gauly2013], has left unaddressed.

The remainder of the paper is structured as follows. The next section details the data sources, including yield and weather records, and outlines the definition of the alfalfa growing season and the construction of degree-day variables. The methods section develops the model relating heat exposure to yield and describes the procedure for simulating future warming scenarios. The results section presents the nonlinear temperature-yield relationship and the geographically differentiated impacts of uniform warming. The discussion then situates these findings within the broader context of climate adaptation, shifts in livestock production, and potential crop switching under the Ricardian framework. The final section provides concluding remarks.
